author: @detch01
text: Be careful what you believe. When much more than half of scientific papers published claim results that are not repeatable, and this is across the sciences, it's a pretty good indicator that any so-called fusion successes need to be investigated thoroughly, without the participation of the individuals and organizations involved with the original claims. Fusion power, as near as I can tell, is still a ways away from being anything even indicating a useful power source might be there. It's all theoretical, math and faith at this point.

author: @Maxjoker98
text: It is a great fusion breakthrough, but not for fusion as an energy source. AFAIK Laser fusion like this won't ever be a reasonable way to produce energy. No, the NIF is interested in fusion for fusion bombs - Better Nukes. Really, what they want to investigate is the hot plasma surrounding a fusion bomb. A lot of the plasma physics research can help with "civil" fusion as well, but I don't think that's the driver behind that particular research project.

author: @pyalot
text: The NIF does not do energy production research. They are a pentagon funded military nuclear weapons research facility. Inertial confinement fusion has no viable path to ever lead to a working energy producing reactor. Also the headline was hugely misleading, because they got more energy released than the lasers deposited on the target pellet. In order to make and transfer that laser energy, they used 50x as much energy as they „got out“. Big burger of nothing.

author: @guytech7310
text:
Come on Robert! You're smarted than that!
1. NIF experiment used 330 MJ to produce about 2.5 MJ of energy. Laser efficiency is very low. 
2. It takes a full day in setup to do a single shot as the pellet alignment has to be perfect. The Even the temperature of the lenses & mirrors has to be extremely controlled since if a mirror is lense position deviates a micrometer, it won't produce any fusion.
3. NIF is a weapons program to create 4th Gen. Nuclear weapons, that don't need a fission core to work. Its never intended to produce electricity as its impossible (see items 1 &2)

Robert, Fusion as a power source will never work. Even if the unity & stability problems could be solved, the costs would be too high. A fully 1GWe fusion plant would costs about $150B USD to build, and about $1B in Tritium daily fuel cost. ITER will cost about $32T for a 500 MwTh reactor with no means to extract electricity & no breader blanket to convert neutrons into Tritium fuel. The cost to make a Breeder Blanket would cost at least $25B, and require about 3 to 4 years of global beryllium annual production just for the ITER reactor.

author: @IslandHermit
text: NIF is only talking about the energy that the lasers delivered to the target, not the energy that was consumed in generating the laser pulses. When you take that into account they are still far from break-even. Even once they get there, they still have to figure out a way to collect the energy released, which will involve even more losses. So true break-even is still a long way away. I expect that ITER will get there years before NIF.

text: The laser requires far more energy in than the fusion made, about 0.5% of the electricity in comes out as the third harmonic beam that actually hits the target. That extreme pressure of the laser light on gold would case conditions similar to that found in the center of a nuclear weapon shortly after detonation.

author: @DKFX1
text: Hey robert. I found out there is a way to increase the face count in tinkercad for cylinder and round models , so that the models become way less blocky when scaled up. You can either search for high res sphere in the search box, and perhaps more cleverly, set side count to 60, duplicate and rotate a cylinder 3, 1.5, 0.75 degrees to the desired detail, then combine them into a single model and save that as a template. Would do wonders for the look of your clever designs,  even if it may add a few minutes of time designing.

author: @thebigreddub
text:
I'm sorrry to have to be the burster of bubbles, but the method used by the folks at the NIF, while it is producing more energy than is put into it, can only produce that energy in short bursts. If you want to use fusion to power peoples homes, the energy output has to be maintained for long periods of time and that's just not feasable with this method.

Nuclear fision on the other hand, has been in commercial use for decades, is statistically the safest form of electricity production, is carbon neutral and, very importantly, is a continuous power source, which we can we easily vary the output from, giving it a huge leg up on wind and solar, which are both intermittent. Nuclear fision reactors are currently the best way we have of producing electricity and we should spend less time obsessing over incremental improvements to a technology which we  might be able to use to produce clean, safe and abundent electricity 30 years from now, and more time advocating for the expansion of the technology that can currently produce clean, safe and abundnet electricity.

replies.author: @bexhill8777
replies.text: Safe! Chernoble? Fucashima? and many others...

replies.author: @galaxya40s95
replies.text:
​@bexhill8777 Afaik there are no cherbobyl style reactors anymore. 
Fukushima was caused by a natural disaster and admittedly badly placed.
Compared to the adverse effects of fossil fuels the fission is green and safe if done right. And would be much better if we spent so much money on perfecting it as we did on fusion.

replies.author: @thebigreddub
replies.text:
@bexhill8777 Chernobyl was pretty bad but 1) That happened 40 years ago, reactor designs have moved on and become considerably safer since then and 2) The reactor in Chernobyl was poorly built and poorly managed even for the time. The exact same fault that occured at Chernobyl occured in the 3-Mile-Island reactor but that meltdown was contained and no one died as a result.

The accident at the Fukushima Daiichi plant only happened after the plant was hit by an earthquake and 2 tsunamis in quick succession. Again, the leak was contained and no one died as a result. The Fukushima excluion has now even been repopulated because the radiation levels have fallen back to well within safe level. Again, Chernobyl was a uniquely bad situation.

The general safety of different types of electricity production is measured in deaths per TWh. Current estimates put electricity produced from nuclear power plants at 0.03 deaths per TWh. Solar is slightly safer at 0.02 and Wind is slightly more dangerous at 0.04. But we're really splitting hairs there, wind, solar and nuclear are the 3 safest forms of electricity generation and we can say they're all about equally safe.

Natural gas is the safest fossil fuel by a good margin and it's the one most commonly used for generating electricity in the west, but even natural gas causes 2.82 deaths per TWh making it 94x as dangerous as nuclear power. You could say that natural gas is astonishingly dangerous but since natural gas is the norm right now, I prefer to say that nuclear is astonishingly safe. 94x safer than natural gas, 614x safer than oil and 821x safer than coal.

replies.author: @thebigreddub

replies.author: @galaxya40s95
replies.text:
This is an issue with terminology. 
The energy in and out is talking about energy required to run the system vs the energy the system produces. The energy contained in the fuel is not part of the comparison.

It is like using a match to light a candle. You put little heat in and get a lot of heat out, at the expense of the fuel in the candle.
The issue is, fusion candles are damn hard to light up and keep burning.

author: @andrewharpin6749
text:
While it is an achievement and I'm sure you understand this better than most Robert.

The issue is due to the nature of the way the device works their Qfusion figures are higher than some other approaches in this instance 1.53, but not double. 

I would personally argue that Qfusion is great for science news, but us "normies" living in the real world, Qtotal is much more relevant.

Their Qtotal is about 0.01 with 1 being break even.

The best for Qtotal is JET at about 0.67.

Don't get me wrong it's a great achievement, but it's still a long way from a commercial reactor.

replies.author: @pinlap3875
replies.text:
I came here to say this. Not mentioning Qtotal causes a lot of confusion.  

It's certainly a momentous achievement (in 2013 their equivalent gain was 0.0077, now it's >1) but at the same time, not all fusion projects are about power generation, many are necessarily about improving technical understanding - with this one I think the lesson ended up being about making the fuel capsule more spherical and able to take more focused laser energy (IIRC).

I read on reddit and Science that the reaction used micrograms of fuel and produced 0.875kWh (boiling a full electric kettle takes 0.19 kWh), but the capacitor banks that stored the energy to drive the pulse supply about 400MJ (111kWh) to generate a 4MJ IR laser pulse, which produces around 2MJ UV laser. An average UK home uses about 10kWh/day, and the NIF experiment could do about 1 fusion experiment per day.  For me that starts to give the scale of the Qtotal challenge.

author: @patkelley4071
text: Overblown hype. The 2.4 megajoule laser input that resulted in a 3.6 megajoule fusion result was powered by 300 megajoule input from the power grid, so the fusion that resulted represented slightly over 1% of the real input power, instead of the ballyhooed "breakthrough" that would lead the gullible listener to think the result was better than 100% of input power.

replies.author: @breakablec
replies.text: Sure and I don't think NIF will ever achieve a wall plug break even due to the quality of the laser beam and efficiency being at odds with each other. However consider that over a few decades fusion reactor maximum efficiency increased millions of times. As such another approach, for example a rebco tokamak can get us there in a reasonable time.

replies.author: @turkeytrac1
replies.text: Well that's your opinion, and like bellybuttons, everyone has one. But here's the thing you miss in dissing it. They're are at the point where at the point of initial reaction they've passed the more out than more in. And that's a good start. Once they can do this regularly, they can widen their approach and start on lessening the input energy needed outside of initial reaction. Yep, not a silver bullet, but you should remember this, Alexander Graham Bell, the Wright Brothers both made many experiments and worked for many many years with both many failures and some successes to get to their major breakthroughs.

replies.author: @patkelley4071
replies.text: ​@turkeytrac1 the media, as usual, made more of this than was warranted, and while it was a kind of milestone for the Livermore scientists, they were not happy with what they felt was exaggeration, and published "corrections" to make the same point you do, but of course the media didn't give the corrections near the attention as the original "blockbuster" hype. The numbers I quoted were real, not opinion, BTW.

replies.author: @analog_guy
replies.text:
Yes, the hype was greatly overblown.  Just consider: 3.6 megajoules of energy is the equivalent of the energy in about three hundredths of a gallon of gasoline.  I'm paying about three dollars for a gallon of gasoline, so 3.6 megajoules is the energy released by about nine cents worth of gasoline.  I wonder how much the gold-plated hohlraum, the extremely precise diamond spherical fuel capsule, and the tritium/deuterium fuel cost??  Those are obliterated in each shot. 🙁 (Not to mention the costs of the facility that is larger than a football field, as well as the other operating costs.)  Rube Goldberg is jumping from his grave for joy! 🙂

Here are the ugly calculations for curious nerds like me:  A gallon of gasoline contains 33.7 kilowatt hours of energy, per the EPA.  Energy of 33.7 kilowatt hours x 1000 watts per kilowatt x 60 minutes per hour x 60 seconds per minute = 121 million watt-seconds = 121 megajoules.  3.6/121 = an energy ratio of 0.03.  A cost of $3.00 per gallon X 0.03 = 9 cents.

replies.author: @analog_guy
replies.text:
@turkeytrac1 I'm sorry, your examples paint a different picture.  Bell and Watson worked for two years from the time of realizing the principle of the telephone to the time of the first working model.  In the following year, telephone lines were being installed, regularly used, and commercial telephone companies were being started.  In the case of Orville and Wilbur Wright, they became interested in pursuing flight 1896, but it was 1899 when they worked with wing-warping kites for flight control.  By the end of 1903 they were successfully flying a motor-powered airplane, so we can say they invested four years (perhaps a bit more) of "serious" effort including the kite work, glider flights, wind tunnel tests, and building the motorized airplane for the first flight.  Both of these inventions rapidly became commercial successes.

On the other hand, the reality of fusion was recognized and determined to be the energy source of the sun in the 1930's.  By the early 1950's, we had fusion bombs.  Since that time, it has taken many thousands of people and untold billions of dollars to get to the point of getting an amount of energy out (about nine cents worth) that was only about 3/2 of the energy that was actually absorbed in the target.  Specifically, the construction of the National Ignition Facility (NIF) began in 1997 so this has consumed 25 years of effort by thousands of people.  The NIF has other purposes as well, such as assuring nuclear weapon safety and reliability, but the leadership claimed over two decades back that they would achieve energy breakeven in a decade.  When they made their big announcement over a year ago, they said they expected to duplicate or exceed the results of that test on a monthly basis.  So far, they have announced only one test that yielded any higher energy, as far as I have heard.  Many orders of magnitude of improvement need to be made before this method could be termed a commercial success.  Many observers believe this particular method of fusion is a dead end to commercially viable power.

Update:  I just heard that the NIF has made a total of four shots that yielded more energy out than went into the fuel pellets, encompassing a period of over a year.  The total fusion energy released over this period was 12.83 megajoules.  That's the equivalent energy of 0.11 gallons of gasoline, which can be purchased for about 33 cents at today's price of $3.00 per gallon.  Wow-wee!  Now all we have to do is convert that energy into some useful form.  (We will just forget about the 1200 megajoules required by the lasers to make the shots, and we will just ignore all the other power used at the NIF and its operating costs.)

I'm not dismissing the gallant effort at the NIF.  I'm recognizing the excessive hype and the misleading claims.

author: @CCoburn3
text:
This is a big breakthrough. But not as big as the fusion researchers want you to think. In order to work, this process depends on tritium. The world's supply of tritium is 16 pounds -- 7.3 kg. Now, we can make more of this stuff.  (We're not going to extract it from seawater. The concentration of tritium in seawater is so low that they had to invent a special unit of measurement because handing the leading zeros was just too cumbersome.) You can make tritium by putting lithium in a fission reactor. Even THAT doesn't make much. (there are other ways to make tritium in a nuclear reactor, but it appears that the lithium method is favored these days.)

The point is that fusion is being sold as super-abundant clean energy that has none of the drawbacks of fission. People like to believe that when these fusion plants get up and running, we will be able to dip a bucket into the sea and power the world for a year. (I know none of the viewers of this channel believe that, but it is an idea that many people have.) People are going to be disappointed when they find that their governments have spent hundreds of billions (if not trillions) on a project that will NOT power the world and which has as many (if not more) problems as uranium reactors.

They might even ask whether that money could have been spent in a better way. Of course, the answer is yes -- we could have built enough small fission (EDIT: Changed fusion to fission here -- typos happen) reactors to power a good bit of the world on what has been used to get experimental fusion to "break even."

replies.author: @ericmc6482
replies.text: Thanks for clarifying, very well said.

replies.author: @LeoTheComm
replies.text: Agreed on the hype, there's an awful lot of BS being sold to the general public. Right now it looks like tritium is the material of choice for experimentation but that's certainly not to say that more abundant material won't be tried in the future. I'm not going to write off fusion as a pipe dream but think of it this way, what would be your perspective on the future applications of electricity if Ben Franklin just published his first paper on the subject of electricity today? That really is where we're at with fusion, so early in the game that we can't fathom what it could turn into.

We could HAVE clean, cheap, abundant energy now for the money we've spent on fusion research. Instead, we've spent it on a project that cannot even provide the power to light a single lightbulb and won't do so for at least 20 years. 

It's as if during a famine, instead of planting the seeds to grow a  good crop we can live on, we used the seed for research into a new type of crop that might produce better food several years down the road.

replies.author: @bmobert
replies.text:
It's a funny joke, to be sure. I laughed.

However, there are open source.plans for building your own fusion reactor and instructions to go along with it. Its called a Farnsworth fusor and was invented and demonstrated in the 1930s.Thats right: we had fusion reactors long before fisison reactors. 

Of course you won't get more energy than you expend, but that's not the only reason you might want fusion. You can design it for neurons or gamma rays or x-rays. Or you can do it for fun.

 Naturally, you should know the dangers of the resultant radiation before beginning, but that's not as hard as you might expect. Thousands have done it over the decades. 

Famously, a US high-school student successfully made and ran one in his parents garage. It was international news for a while. 

There's a YouTuber that focuses on plasmas that started a project to make a desktop Farnsworth fusor. To my knowledge, he's not finished it, but I'm sure you can search YouTube for the vids.

There are other home fusion reactor designs besides the Farnsworth  as well. There was a fellow in New York state, if I recall correctly, who experimented with a home-built Polyewell fusor. I never heard what finally happened with him... Another fellow in Australia made a Polywell fusor in Uni, and I believe he wrote and published a scientific paper on the subject. (I could be wrong about the paper. I believe it's true, but I could be misremembering.)

Regardless, there are homemade fusion reactors out there, some more effective than others. None make power. Some are useful machines, doing useful work. Most make pretty, pretty plasma shows. All are interesting.

replies.text: @myacctoostale9345 Yes, but fission is no more problematic from a radioactive waste standpoint. And what most people don't realize is that to produce the fuel for fusion reactors, you have to run fission reactors. So why spend TREMENDOUS amounts of money developing a technology when existing technology will fill the same need? For the money we've spent just reaching the "break-even" point with fusion, we could have built thousands (if not tens of thousands) of small fission reactors that would be meeting our energy needs NOW -- instead of 20 years into the future.

replies.author: @gaiustesla9324
replies.text:
@michaelfixedsys7463 "Polonium is one of the most toxic substances known. According to some sources, it is up to a trillion times more toxic than hydrogen cyanide."

" Ingesting just 50 ng or inhaling 10 ng of polonium can cause death" thats 50 billionth's of a gram. 

"1989 – In the Kramatorsk radiological accident, a small capsule containing highly radioactive 137 Cs was found inside the concrete wall in an apartment building in Kramatorsk, Ukrainian SSR. It is believed that the capsule, originally contained in a measurement device, was lost sometime during the late 1970s and ended up mixed in with gravel used to construct that building in 1980. By the time the capsule was discovered, four residents of the building had died from leukemia and 17 more received varying doses of radiation."

"In 1993, director of the Kartontara packing company Vladimir Kaplun was killed by radioactive material (cobalt-60 and/or cesium-137) placed in his chair. He died of radiation sickness after a month of hospitalization. The source of the radiation was found after his death"

"An unnamed truck driver was killed by five months of radiation exposure to a 1.3 curies (48 GBq) caesium-137 source (about 15 mg) that had been put into the door of his truck around February 1995. He died of radiation-induced leukemia on 27 April 1997"

etc etc etc.....
